risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were then randomized either to topical 1% testosterone gel (10-g daily) or placebo gel daily in 1:1 allocation during a 14-week intervention period using concealed randomization and permuted blocks with a block size of 4." Comment: The study explicitly describes a structured randomization procedure using permuted blocks, which is a recognized random sequence generation method and provides sufficient detail to support truly random allocation.

Quote: "Participants were then randomized either to topical 1% testosterone gel (10-g daily) or placebo gel daily in 1:1 allocation during a 14-week intervention period using concealed randomization and permuted blocks with a block size of 4." Comment: The paper states that randomization was "concealed," but does not explicitly describe the concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, pharmacy-controlled assignment). Although the investigational drug pharmacist knew assignment, the report does not clearly state that the pharmacist controlled allocation before enrollment in a way that prevented foreknowledge by recruiters. Therefore allocation concealment cannot be confirmed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The investigating staff and the participants were blinded to intervention allocation; the group and testosterone dose assignment was known only to the Investigational Drug Pharmacist, who dispensed testosterone gel and sildenafil, and a designated physician, who performed the dose titration. To maintain blinding, the participants received 3-tubes of the gel daily; those assigned to the 10-g dose received daily two tubes containing testosterone gel and one tube containing the placebo gel, while those assigned to placebo group received three tubes of the placebo gel." Comment: Participants and key study personnel were explicitly stated to be blinded, with matching placebo procedures used to maintain the blind. Although one physician was unblinded for dose titration, the paper states this person "did not participate in any other aspects of the trial," so blinding of participants and personnel delivering/conducting the intervention appears adequate. Because the outcomes include subjective self-reported measures, blinding is important, and the reported methods support low risk rather than high or unclear risk.

Quote: "The investigating staff and the participants were blinded to intervention allocation; the group and testosterone dose assignment was known only to the Investigational Drug Pharmacist, who dispensed testosterone gel and sildenafil, and a designated physician, who performed the dose titration." and "Well-being and mood were assessed using the Psychological General Well-Being Index (PGWBI), a 22-item questionnaire..." and "The Derogatis Affects Balance Scale (DABS) is a 40-item mood inventory..." Comment: The most vulnerable outcomes were subjective patient-reported questionnaires (PGWBI, DABS; also the parent trial's sexual-function outcomes were self-reported). Here, the patient is the outcome assessor, but participants were explicitly blinded to allocation. Investigating staff were also blinded, and the only unblinded physician was restricted to dose titration and "did not participate in any other aspects of the trial." Therefore knowledge of allocation was unlikely to bias outcome measurement.

Quote: "140 men were randomized either to sildenafil and testosterone gel (n=70) or sildenafil and placebo gel (n=70). In the testosterone arm, 5 were lost to follow-up, 2 withdrew due to skin irritation, 2 were discontinued due to developing hematocrit exceeding 54%, and 1 withdrew due to the smell of the gel. In the placebo arm, 11 were lost to follow up and 1 withdrew due to skin irritation. Thus, 60 and 58 participants in the testosterone and placebo arms, respectively, completed the trial." and "9.7% of PGWBI and DABS scores were imputed due to missingness." Comment: Post-randomization attrition for the analysed mood/well-being outcomes over 14 weeks was 10/70 (14.3%) in the testosterone arm and 12/70 (17.1%) in the placebo arm, an absolute difference of 2.8 percentage points. Reasons are reported and there is no major imbalance; although some testosterone-arm discontinuations were for adverse effects, overall loss is moderate and balanced. The analysis also used multiple imputation for missing outcome records, which is an appropriate approach for this level of missingness. Therefore incomplete outcome data are unlikely to have materially biased the outcome comparison.
